author: @toutlemonde.1179
text:
Good morning Robert.
Great video.
I am trying to make a dual use device, a kettle/hotplate and I came across your video which is very well explained.
I am new to this so I just have a few quick questions for you:
1- you mentioned in this video that the copper melts with the heat in the switch and thus the power gets turned off, so, if the copper has melted, how does the switch operate a second time after this?
2- is capton tape the best material avaible for the use it is put to n this video, or would there be a better material that does the job a bit better?
3- is the metal to heat used in a kettle and toaster the same material and would their be a better material that I could use for this job?
I hope to make a little dual kettle/hotplate.
To be honest-I wish they taught practical, usefull information like this in school but thank God for YouTube and teachers you!
(I hope you don't mind me calling you a teacher, but in that few minutes I did learn a lot of really applicable information to solve my problem at hand. I'm looking forward to checking out the rest of your videos.
Good man,thank you.)

author: @CASHSEC
text: Where is the construction part of this work?

author: @BluesDoctor
text: Always great to see a 'new to me' video you have done. Maybe one of these days I will make it through them all. Cheers

author: @grumpydog2891
text: how will the ink hold up to constant use as say a hot plate / heater using say 350 degree temps

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: should be fine - i would coat the top with kapton though - that would allow you to take it up to 400 degrees

author: @GreenmeResearch
text:
Anybody here also actually replicating this?
Would like to be in contact with others about this.
We have bought the ink and all other components.

author: @applesworld
text: p.s. of course being flat glass, you could mount it on the 'outside' back of the tank glass, not in the water itself. Perhaps ( since required fish tank temps are rarely above 28 degrees Celsius ) the 'element' could be laminated with a coloured ( deep blue or black being popular ) or a photographic tank decor sheet.

author: @applesworld
text:
Robert ! that's great!
My first thought was ( in insulated sheet form ) a large fish tank heating element covering the entire back of a ( flat glassed ) fish tank to give an even heating source and it would also negate the need for an ugly traditional aquarium heater stuck on the back of the glass.

I stumbled across your video's from watching electro-plating how-to's and I must say, you and your video's are a revelation!

author: @Tronman63
text: This might be a stupid question because never studied electronics but could this ink some how benefit a solar cooker?

author: @bullseye9899
text: Robert..  As mentioned in an earlier post....  If making a 3d printer build plate would you see any benefits as in faster heating with similar 12 volt supply and 8 x 8 inch surface area?  Also I assume this process needs a non conductive surface correct?  Thanks for the videos.  You have a lot of great ideas!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - to make it truly non conductive - i would cover it with kapton - that'll take the heat too

replies.author: @bullseye9899
replies.text:
My curiosity has got the better of me... I don't want to take up much of your time, but here is what I am thinking....
I am using one of the generic circuit board heaters at 12 volts to heat a 1/8" thick 8" x 8" square piece of 6061 aluminum.  The extruder uses a 40 watt cartridge heater at 12 volts.  The extruder can reach its operating temp of 230 C in about 5 mins.  The bed takes 8 min to get to 70 C for printing PLA.  When I go to ABS  the bed needs to heat up to over 90 C  which takes over 20 mins.  Would it be possible to coat the aluminum plate with conductive ink at the correct resistance and decrease the time to warm it up?  Maybe even coat both sides to double heating surfaces?  I'd like to stay with 12 volts and my current power supply is 450 watts.  Am I on the right track or am I missing something?  what are your thoughts?  Thanks for your time and wonderful ideas.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sounds doable - but i would mix the ink with bentonite clay to adjust the resistance and therefor heating rate - but - increasing heating rate is going to increase power draw until the temp you want is reached then draw will be drastically reduced as all you are doing is maintaining temp against losses

author: @das250250
text:
Love your work Robert you are the embodiment of a true innovator  ... :
This INK ( which I contributed to btw ) could be used to heat water from a solar system. Alternatively can be used for a load resistor , convection heating .
Keep up the fantastic stuff ,we need a work-group for people to do this stuff :-)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - a workgroup would be good

author: @africanelectron751
text: It would be a great method to heat the printing surface of diy 3d printers. keep up the good work

replies.author: @bullseye9899
replies.text: I thought the same thing....  3d printer plate.... Have to do the math,  but I  think there would not be any real benefits over a regular resistance heat bed.   After all the power equations for both systems are the same.   It would still be cool though.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Ben Rogerson
you are quite right - there wouldn't be any functional benefit - but you may be missing the point - with this ink - you could paint your own lol - however, your 100% right on it would be v cool - it would be super cool lol

replies.author: @bullseye9899
replies.text: Absolutely!  I am still going to try it out.   Am I correct that this should be done on a non-conductive surface... Thanks

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's a resistive heater mate,so it will conduct but the conduction is minimal - think toaster - I mean it is pretty safe - it touch it in the video after all - but you wouldn't want to put metals on it.

text: This would be useful as a heating pad for the purpose of starting seedlings and especially for rooting cuttings.
